run-llama/llama_index#20025.body:
### Question Validation

- [x] I have searched both the documentation and discord for an answer.

### Question

It could very well just be me, but I find the new automatically generated changelog/release notes to be a bit harder to "follow", especially when it comes to the actual commits that are involved in a "release".

For example, **comparing the change list for `llama-index-llms-bedrock-converse`**, the latest notes for the [v0.14.4 release](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/releases/tag/v0.14.4) ([CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/97200368386f8d460151dc4bad2fa5af8540cf47/CHANGELOG.md#llama-index-llms-bedrock-converse-095)) from `2025-10-03` seem to _not_ list "intermediate" commits under each sub-package (only lists the "latest" commit?), whereas the notes for the [v0.12.38 release](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/releases/tag/v0.12.38) ([CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/97200368386f8d460151dc4bad2fa5af8540cf47/CHANGELOG.md#llama-index-llms-bedrock-converse-060)) from `2025-05-28` _do_ list "intermediate" commits.

To look at it a bit differently, notice how the [v0.14.4 release](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/releases/tag/v0.14.4) only lists 1 commit under `llama-index-llms-bedrock-converse [0.9.5]`, which is:
- feat: Additional support for Claude Sonnet 4.5 (https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/pull/19980)

but doesn't list the previous commits (example):
- Feat/bedrock converse partial system prompt cache (https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/pull/19922) (bumped version to `0.9.4`)
- feat: add anthropic sonnet 4.5 (https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/pull/19977) (bumped version to `0.9.3`)

Definitely not a big deal, and I do suppose it could be a bit tricky to get right in the monorepo setting, but figured I'd open this issue for the discussion as I found the old format a bit nicer for tracking purposes 😅
run-llama/llama_index#20025.title: [Question]: Commits missing in new automated changelog/release notes

run-llama/llama_index#20035.body:
### Question Validation

- [x] I have searched both the documentation and discord for an answer.

### Question

So we have this problem where despite all of our code being written in async there is still something that seems to be not async and we believe we've narrowed it down to either the `AmazonKnowledgeBaseRetriever` or `TreeSummarize`.

The problem is the array of async events becomes blocking and then our asyncio/fastapi server goes down.

Our code looks like this:

```
# retriever config
retrieval_config = {
            "vectorSearchConfiguration": {
                "numberOfResults": similarity_top_k,
                "overrideSearchType": "HYBRID",
                "filter": {
                    "in": {
                        "key": "...",
                        "value": ...
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        # Create bedrock retriever
        bedrock_retriever = AmazonKnowledgeBasesRetriever(
            knowledge_base_id=site_settings.AWS_KNOWLEDGE_BASE_ID,
            retrieval_config=retrieval_config,
            region_name="us-east-1",
            aws_access_key_id=site_settings.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID,
            aws_secret_access_key=site_settings.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY,
        )

        return QueryFusionRetriever(
            retrievers=[bedrock_retriever],
            similarity_top_k=similarity_top_k,
            mode=FUSION_MODES.RECIPROCAL_RANK,
            num_queries=num_queries,
        )

async def query_item(...):
  # use TreeSummarize for synthesizer
  # use QueryFusionRetriever (based on AmazonKnowledgeBaseRetriever) to make query_engine
  # call query_engine.aquery(...)

tasks = [
  query_item(...),
  for item in items
]

task_res = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
```
run-llama/llama_index#20035.title: [Question]: Is AmazonKnowledgeBaseRetriever synchronous?

from llama_index.readers.gitlab import GitLabRepositoryReader
import gitlab

gl = gitlab.Gitlab(url=GITLAB_URL, private_token=TOKEN)
reader = GitLabRepositoryReader(gitlab=gl, project_id=PROJECT_ID)
documents = reader.load_data(ref="main")

run-llama/llama_index#20055.body:
### Bug Description

Currently the `_is_efficient_filtering_enabled` function in the `OpensearchVectorClient` class checks to see if the major version of opensearch is greater than or equal to 2 AND if the minor version is greater than 9.

This causes an issue when running the latest versions of opensearch (3.2 is latest release at time of writing) as the minor release version is less than 9. The minor release version should only be checked if the major release is equal to 2.

### Version

0.14.4

### Steps to Reproduce

Initialise vector store client with opensearch 3.x deployment

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```

This is a follow-up on #20072: now the tool calling happens correctly but sometime some of the text deltas get streamed twice. This happens for both thinking and not thinking models (tested with Claude 4.5 Sonnet)

This is my code: 
```
def get_weather(location: str) -> str:
    """
    Get the current weather for a given location.
    """
    # This is a placeholder implementation. Replace with actual weather fetching logic.
    return f"The current weather in {location} is sunny with a temperature of 25°C."
 
def gcp_claude_4_5_sonnet_thinking() -> Anthropic:
    llm = Anthropic(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5@20250929",
        project_id=os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_PROJECT_ID"),

        additional_kwargs={"max_tokens": 64000, "temperature": 1},
        timeout=240,

    )
    Settings.llm = llm
    return llm

get_weather = FunctionTool.from_defaults(
    name="get_weather",
    description="Get the current weather for a given location.",
    fn=get_weather,
)

llm = Models.gcp_claude_4_5_sonnet_thinking()
agent = FunctionAgent(
    llm=llm # claude sonnet 4.5 thinking,
    tools=[get_weather]
    )
ctx = Context(agent)

handler = agent.run(
    "What is the weather in San Francisco?",
    context=ctx)

async for ev in handler.stream_events():

        if ev.delta:

run-llama/llama_index#20102.body:
### Question Validation

- [x] I have searched both the documentation and discord for an answer.

### Question

Hi,

I'm using LlamaIndex's `FunctionAgent` with both Ollama LLM and Anthropic LLM. With Ollama, the agent calls the tool and then generates a response to the user as expected. With Anthropic LLM, the tool call is made successfully and the tool result is returned, but after that, the agent does not generate a follow-up response. There are no errors or warnings—the process just stops after the tool result.

**What I’ve checked**:
- The tool call and tool result are handled correctly.
- The same workflow works with Ollama LLM.
- No exceptions or errors are raised.
- Using llama-index-llms-anthropic v0.9.5.

Messages sent to Anthropic (printed just before the response object):
```python
anthropic_messages, system_prompt = messages_to_anthropic_messages(messages, self.cache_idx)
# Printed anthropic_messages here
```

Example (anthropic_messages):
```python
user- 'content': [
    {'type': 'text', 'text': 'Hello'}
]
assistant- 'content': [
    {'type': 'text', 'text': "Hello! I'm **AI Assistant**, your Digital Cooking Agent. \n\nHow can I help you today?"}
]
user- 'content': [
    {'type': 'text', 'text': 'I want to cook eggs today. how can i do that?'}
]
assistant- 'content': [
    {'id': 'toolu_01XpPXWym1Nu8W3XbrJEvBQf', 'input': {'cooking': {'to_be_cooked': 'eggs'}}, 'name': 'start_cooking', 'type': 'tool_use'}, 
    {'id': 'toolu_01TqML4SacyqDNzmh9M1s21H', 'input': {}, 'name': 'get_current_cooking_status', 'type': 'tool_use'}
]
user- 'content': [
    {'tool_use_id': 'toolu_01XpPXWym1Nu8W3XbrJEvBQf', 'type': 'tool_result', 'content': [{'type': 'text', 'text': "{'cooking': {'to_be_cooked': 'eggs', 'chilly': None, 'pepper': None, 'salt': None, 'oil': None}}"}]}, 
    {'tool_use_id': 'toolu_01TqML4SacyqDNzmh9M1s21H', 'type': 'tool_result', 'content': [{'type': 'text', 'text': "{'cooking': {'to_be_cooked': 'eggs', 'chilly': None, 'pepper': None, 'salt': None, 'oil': None}}"}]}
]
```

After this, the response object is not generated and nothing happens.

Is there something different about how Anthropic expects tool results to be formatted or streamed back? Any guidance would be appreciated!
run-llama/llama_index#20102.title: [Question]: Why does FunctionAgent not generate a response after tool call with Anthropic LLM (but works with Ollama)?

run-llama/llama_index#20109.body:
### Feature Description

creating a mcp tool with an argument that is a union of something with a Literal is mis-resolved (is that a word?).
not sure this is a bug or feature, since the type resolution is clearly not "universal". some form of recusion seems missing, but not sure that is wanted or not (and clearly far from trivial).

currently fixed by monkeypatching
```
from llama_index.tools.mcp.tool_spec_mixins import TypeResolutionMixin
orig_resolve_union_option = TypeResolutionMixin._resolve_union_option
def new_resolve_union_option(
        self: "McpToolSpec",
        option: dict,
        defs: dict,
    ) -> Any:
    if "enum" in option:
        return Literal[tuple(option["enum"])]
    return orig_resolve_union_option(self, option, defs)
TypeResolutionMixin._resolve_union_option = new_resolve_union_option
```

but that is copy/pasting the enum->Literal code from _resolve_field_type; probably not what you want

### Reason

certain mcp tools en up missing the allowed/possible fields when being used through llama_index

### Value of Feature

_No response_
run-llama/llama_index#20109.title: [Feature Request]: `llama_index.tools.mcp.tool_spec_mixins` `TypeResolutionMixin` to support union of Literal

run-llama/llama_index#20110.body:
### Feature Description

Add Airweave tool integration to enable agents to search across data synced from 30+ sources.

Airweave is an open-source platform that automatically syncs data from multiple sources (Google Drive, Notion, GitHub, databases, etc.) and provides unified search with advanced retrieval.

### Reason

Don't think anyone has ever taken the time to build it, we will open a PR with working integration.

### Value of Feature

- Instant access to 30+ pre-built data source connectors for agent builders
- Automatic syncing keeps data up-to-date without manual pipelines
- Advanced search (hybrid retrieval, temporal relevance, reranking)
- Built-in RAG answer generation
- Open-source and self-hostable

Perfect for enterprise knowledge bases where data needs to stay continuously synced.
run-llama/llama_index#20110.title: [Feature Request]: Add Airweave Integration as a Tool

When I tried to use DocumentBlock inside ChatMessage, I got the following error `BadRequestError: Error code: 400 - {'error': {'message': "Invalid value: 'input_file'. Supported values are: 'text', 'image_url', 'input_audio', 'refusal', 'audio', and 'file'.", 'type': 'invalid_request_error', 'param': 'messages[0].content[1].type', 'code': 'invalid_value'}}`

    """Useful function to add two numbers."""
    return x + y


def multiply(x: int, y: int) -> int:
    """Useful function to multiply two numbers."""
    return x * y


tools = [
    FunctionTool.from_defaults(add),
    FunctionTool.from_defaults(multiply),
]

    llm=OpenAI(model="gpt-4o"), tools=tools, timeout=120, verbose=True
)

    role="user",
    blocks=[
        TextBlock(text="summarize the following document"),
        DocumentBlock(path="./data/doc2.pdf"),

)

run-llama/llama_index#20128.body:
### Feature Description

### Current Limitation

The PostgreSQL vector store's `indexed_metadata_keys` parameter currently only supports scalar types (text, int, float, etc.) and does not support array types like `text[]`. This means array-valued metadata fields cannot be efficiently indexed, resulting in slow sequential scans for array membership queries.

### Proposed Feature

Extend `indexed_metadata_keys` to support `text[]` arrays with automatic GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) creation for fast array operations.

**Example Usage:**
```python
vector_store = PGVectorStore.from_params(
    ...,
    indexed_metadata_keys=[
        ("tags", "text[]"),         # Creates GIN index - NEW
        ("categories", "text[]"),   # Creates GIN index - NEW
        ("user_id", "text"),        # Creates BTREE index - existing behavior
    ]
)
This would enable efficient filtering on array metadata using PostgreSQL's native operators:
?| (ANY) - Array contains at least one of the specified values
?& (ALL) - Array contains all of the specified values
@> (CONTAINS) - Array contains a specific value
```

Use Cases

- Tagging systems: Filter documents by tags like ["AI", "ML", "NLP"]
- Multi-tenant applications: Filter by user/tenant arrays
- Content categorization: Hierarchical categories stored as arrays
- Access control: Permission arrays like ["admin", "editor", "viewer"]
- Topic classification: Multiple topics per document


### Reason

**What's blocking this today:**

The current implementation only handles scalar metadata types in `indexed_metadata_keys`. Array types are neither validated nor indexed, forcing users to either:

1. **Store arrays as JSON strings** - Loses type safety and query capabilities
2. **Manually create GIN indices with raw SQL** - Requires DB access, not declarative, error-prone:
   ```python
   # Current workaround (not ideal)
   session.execute(text("""
       CREATE INDEX idx_tags_gin 
       ON table_name USING gin ((metadata_->'tags'))
   """))


### Value of Feature

If you're storing arrays in metadata (tags, categories, etc.), there's no way to create proper indices for them. Right now the `indexed_metadata_keys` parameter only supports scalar types, so array queries are slow.

Common use cases that need this:
- Tag-based filtering (find documents with any/all of these tags)
- Multi-tenant category lookups
- Any metadata that's naturally a list

The fix would be to support `text[]` in `indexed_metadata_keys` and automatically create GIN indices for array fields instead of BTREE. This would follow the same pattern as existing scalar indices, just use the right index type for arrays. LlamaIndex already uses GIN for full-text search, so this would just extend that to user-defined array metadata.
run-llama/llama_index#20128.title: [Feature Request]: Add GIN index support for text array metadata fields in PostgreSQL vector store

run-llama/llama_index#20151.body:
### Feature Description

In a recent update, support for the global cross-region inference profile prefix was added to llama-index-llms-bedrock-converse ([see PR 20064](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/pull/20064)).
Similarly, AWS now provides a "jp" (Japan) cross-region inference profile for Bedrock Claude Sonnet 4.5 and Bedrock Claude Haiku 4.5 models.
I would like to request that llama-index-llms-bedrock-converse support the "jp" cross-region inference profile for these models, allowing users to select the Japan profile as needed.

Reference:
 - AWS official documentation for supported cross-region inference profiles: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/inference-profiles-support.html

### Reason

Currently, while the global cross-region inference profile is supported, there is no way to select or utilize the "jp" (Japan) cross-region profile, which can route requests specifically via the Japanese region's optimized resources.
This limitation is problematic for users and workloads that benefit from region-specific inference, such as compliance requirements, latency optimization, or data residency concerns specific to Japan.

### Value of Feature

Adding support for the "jp" cross-region inference profile would:
 - Enable users to leverage lower latency and regional optimization for workloads specific to Japan.
 - Increase compliance with data residency requirements for Japanese customers.
run-llama/llama_index#20151.title: [Feature Request]: Add support for "jp" cross-region inference profile for Bedrock Claude Sonnet 4.5 and Haiku 4.5

### Bug Description

[According to your docs](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/v0.14.6/docs/src/content/docs/framework/module_guides/deploying/agents/tools.md?plain=1#L60), using `FunctionTool.from_defaults` should automatically use the function schema and docstring to generate a tool description that get's sent to the LLM.

Yes, one could manually provide a description - but re-using an existing python docstring and not managing descriptions in two places is highly preferred to keep the codebase clean and maintainable.

### Version

0.14.3

### Steps to Reproduce

1. have function with multi-line docstring, as it is common
2. use `FunctionTools.from_defaults` without specifiying custom description
3. LLM only gets first summary line

**Error Message**
ValueError: ChatMessage contains multiple blocks, use 'ChatMessage.blocks' instead.

    asyncio.run(orchestrator.run())

    task_development_success, agent_final_response =  await self.dev_agent.execute_task(task)

    output = await self.finalize(ctx, ev, memory)
  File "E:\WORKSPACE\devagents\.venv\Lib\site-packages\llama_index\core\agent\workflow\react_agent.py", line 277, in finalize
    output.response.content = output.response.content[

run-llama/llama_index#20194.body:
### Feature Description

Currently, the llama_index integration with Anthropic models ignores the RawMessageDeltaEvent messages returned from the Anthropic streaming API.
These events contain valuable information such as:

* input / output tokens counters
* stop reason (e.g., end_turn, max_tokens, etc.)


For example, when using OpenAI, it is already possible to access the `finish_reason` from streamed events like this:
```
async for event in handler.stream_events():
    if isinstance(event, AgentOutput):
          if choices: = event.raw.get("choices", None):
              for choice in choices:
                 finish_reason = choice.get("finish_reason", None)   
```

I would like to be able to get this information from anthropic as well


### Reason

_No response_

### Value of Feature

This feature would provide access to important metadata from Anthropic responses — including stop reasons and token/billing usage — that are currently lost when RawMessageDeltaEvent is ignored.

It would:

* Improve observability during streaming calls

* Enable cost and rate-limit tracking

* Help developers debug or optimize model interactions

* Bring the Anthropic integration to feature parity with other LLM providers supported by LlamaIndex (e.g., OpenAI)
run-llama/llama_index#20194.title: [Feature Request]: Support for Anthropic RawMessageDeltaEvent

run-llama/llama_index#20205.body:
### Bug Description

`llama-index-storage-kvstore-postgres v0.4.2` introduces breaking change in privilege requirements

PR #20104 changed `_create_schema_if_not_exists()` to always execute 
`CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS`, which requires the `CREATE` privilege 
even when the schema already exists.

**Impact:**
- Breaks deployments following principle of least privilege
- Database users with only USAGE/ALL on existing schemas now fail
- Released as patch version but should have been breaking change

**Suggested Fix:**
Revert to check-first pattern or use `inspect(engine).get_schema_names()` 
to avoid requiring CREATE privilege when schema exists.


### Version

llama-index-storage-kvstore-postgres v0.4.2

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create schema as superuser
2. Grant USAGE + ALL PRIVILEGES on schema to app user
3. Do NOT grant CREATE on database to app user
4. v0.4.1: ✅ Works
5. v0.4.2: ❌ Fails with "permission denied for database"

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20205.title: [Bug]: llama-index-storage-kvstore-postgres v0.4.2 introduces breaking change in privilege requirements

run-llama/llama_index#20218.body:
### Bug Description

During my development with the `GoogleGenAI` LLM and MLFlow Tracing, I noticed that token usages are not recorded by the MLFlow llama_index tracer. After investigations, the MLFlow llama_index tracer logs the following error as debug log:

```
DEBUG mlflow.llama_index.tracer: Failed to set TokenUsage to the span: 'prompt_tokens'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "...\.venv\Lib\site-packages\mlflow\llama_index\tracer.py", line 454, in _parse_usage
    TokenUsageKey.INPUT_TOKENS: usage["prompt_tokens"],
                                ~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
KeyError: 'prompt_tokens'
```

The MLFlow tracer expects the `prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens` and optionally the `total_tokens` keys to be set in the `additional_kwargs` attribute of `ChatResponse` but are currently missing.

These information must be set by `GoogleGenAI` before returning the response to avoid the error (see the `OpenAI` LLM integration for an example).


### Version

llama-index-llms-google-genai==0.7.1

### Steps to Reproduce

Here is some minimal code to reproduce the error:

```python
import logging
import mlflow
from llama_index.core.base.llms.types import ChatMessage
from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI

# Set the mlflow llama_index tracer logger to DEBUG
logging.getLogger("mlflow.llama_index.tracer").setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

# Activate llama_index autologging in MLFlow
mlflow.llama_index.autolog()

#  Call the LLM
llm = GoogleGenAI(model="gemini-2.5-flash-lite")
response = llm.chat(
    messages=[ChatMessage(role="user", content="What is the capital of France?")]
)
```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20218.title: [Bug]: Missing token usage information in GoogleGenAI metadata for MLFlow Tracing

run-llama/llama_index#20237.body:
### Bug Description

The qdrant store is missing FilterOperators, e.g. `CONTAINS`, `ANY`, ...:

https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/main/llama-index-integrations/vector_stores/llama-index-vector-stores-qdrant/llama_index/vector_stores/qdrant/base.py#L1376

**Expected**: A warning id displayed or a NotImplementedError is thrown when not-handled FilterOperators (e.g. `FilterOperator.CONTAINS`) are used.
**Actual**: not-handled FilterOperators are silently ignored, and an empty filter, matching everything, is returned.

### Version

0.14.7

### Steps to Reproduce

1. create a qdrant store, fill it with some data with metadata { roles: ["admin"] }
2. create a MetadataFilter using `FilterOperator.CONTAINS`:
    `MetadataFilter(key="roles", value="tom", operator=FilterOperator.CONTAINS)`
4. use it in a query_engine, query the engine

**Expected**: no points are found, because the roles don't match: "roles" does not include "tom" for any document/chunk.
**Actual**: all points are found.
run-llama/llama_index#20237.title: [Bug]: qdrant store is missing FilterOperators

run-llama/llama_index#20242.body:
### Bug Description

Currently, `VectorIndexRetriever` will generate a query embedding regardless of the `vector_store_query_mode`, even for "text_search". This probably is not wanted as it is an unnecessary external API call.

It is currently checked with `if self._vector_store.is_embedding_query:`, which will always be True for any vector store supporting vector search - in my case the OpenSearch Vector store. Probably this could be changed to

```
if self._vector_store.is_embedding_query and self._vector_store_query_mode in (VectorStoreQueryMode.DEFAULT, VectorStoreQueryMode.SEMANTIC_HYBRID):
    ...  # calculate query embedding
```

See https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/bc52c85394d9022f7785573c380147e75da87dbd/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/indices/vector_store/retrievers/retriever.py#L92-L104

I realized that my tests for number of expected embedding calls failed when I switched my `QueryFusionRetriever` to async behaviour and noticed this. This is also due to a small difference in behaviour between `QueryFusionRetriever._retrieve` and `QueryFusionRetriever._aretrieve`. While the first will set the query embedding on the first retriever pass and then avoid generating the next embedding, this is not the case in the async call due to the non-sequential nature. This causes the `QueryFusionRetriever` to generate a query embedding for each retriever linked, despite it being the same query.

See https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/bc52c85394d9022f7785573c380147e75da87dbd/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/retrievers/fusion_retriever.py#L245

### Version

llama-index-core==0.12.28

### Steps to Reproduce

Might add example code here later.

1. Create a `VectorIndexRetriever` with `VectorStoreQueryMode.TEXT_SEARCH`
2. Call `_retrieve` or `_aretrieve`. It will create a query embedding.

QueryFusionRetriever:
1. Create a `QueryFusionRetriever` with multiple `VectorIndexRetriever` of different modes
2. Call `_retrieve`. It will create a single query embedding.
3. Call `_aretrieve`. It will create a query embedding for each retriever.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20242.title: [Bug]: VectorIndexRetriever calculates embedding regardless of `vector_store_query_mode`

### Bug Description

If Memory's flushing limit is set low enough such that one conversation turn is enough to make it overflow and trigger the flushing behavior, it is possible to end up with a list of active messages that begin with a assistant message instead of a user message.

Some providers like Amazon Bedrock do not allow this and return an error, since they require that the first message be a user message.

### Version

0.12.52.post1

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create FunctionTool such that the token count returned by the tool triggers Memory's flushing behavior
2. Create any chat
3. Observe that the remaining message in the active queue is only the latest assistant reply

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20256.title: [Bug]: Memory's flushing behavior in _manage_queue may end up with an assistant message as the first message

run-llama/llama_index#20257.body:
### Bug Description

When `AzureAISearchVectorStore` is constructed with BOTH a `SearchIndexClient` and an `AsyncSearchIndexClient` that are managed with context managers, the store internally calls:

`self._async_search_client = self._async_index_client.get_search_client(index_name)`

The `AsyncSearchIndexClient` context manager cleans up its own aiohttp session on exit, but the AsyncSearchClient returned from get_search_client spins up a new aiohttp.ClientSession/TCPConnector that is never closed.

As a result, the asyncio loop logs:

`Asyncio unhandled exception in task 'unknown': Unclosed client session`
and
`Unclosed connector`


To avoid this you have to ad `finally` block which closes the sessions:

```
    with SyncSearchIndexClient(endpoint=endpoint, index_name=index_name, credential=creds) as sync_client:
        async with AsyncSearchIndexClient(endpoint=endpoint, index_name=index_name, credential=creds) as async_client:
            vector_store = AzureAISearchVectorStore(
                search_or_index_client=sync_client,
                async_search_or_index_client=async_client,
                index_name=index_name,
                index_management=IndexManagement.CREATE_IF_NOT_EXISTS,
                id_field_key="id",
                chunk_field_key="chunk",
                embedding_field_key="embedding",
                metadata_string_field_key="metadata",
                doc_id_field_key="doc_id",
            )
          
            try:
                yield vector_store
            finally:
                async_search_client = getattr(vector_store, "_async_search_client", None)
                if async_search_client:
                    await async_search_client.close()
  
                search_client = getattr(vector_store, "_search_client", None)
                if search_client:
                    search_client.close()
```




### Version

0.12.52

### Steps to Reproduce

```
from azure.search.documents import SearchIndexClient
from azure.search.documents.indexes import SearchIndexClient as SyncSearchIndexClient
from azure.search.documents.indexes.aio import SearchIndexClient as AsyncSearchIndexClient
from azure.core.credentials import AzureKeyCredential

            vector_store = AzureAISearchVectorStore(
                search_or_index_client=sync_client,
                async_search_or_index_client=async_client,
                index_name=index_name,
                index_management=IndexManagement.CREATE_IF_NOT_EXISTS,
                id_field_key="id",
                chunk_field_key="chunk",
                embedding_field_key="embedding",
                metadata_string_field_key="metadata",
                doc_id_field_key="doc_id",

    # Exiting the context emits "Unclosed client session" warnings
```

run-llama/llama_index#20264.body:
### Bug Description

I've observed that when calling `as_chat_engine` from `MultiModalVectorStoreIndex`, the corresponding chat engine is not multimodal. More specifically, this issue occurs with `ChatMode.CONTEXT` and `ChatMode.CONDENSE_PLUS_CONTEXT`. 

This is not an issue with `ChatMode.SIMPLE` and `ChatMode.CONDENSE_QUESTION`, as the latter uses query engine internally, which, in turn is multimodal.

I tracked this down to the response synthesizer not being multi-modal within the two chat engines. In particular I looked at the context chat engine, but my testing showed a similar problem with condense plus context.

### Version

v0.14.8

### Steps to Reproduce

To reproduce the problem, take an image containing the solution to a question. Ask the chat engine a question that can only be answered by looking at the picture and observe that the answer is not provided. Alternatively, deep-dive and check the image gets lost in the response synthesizer.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20264.title: [Bug]: MultiModalVectorStoreIndex.as_chat_engine(ChatMode.CONTEXT | ChatMode.CONDENSE_PLUS_CONTEXT) does not yield a multi-modal chat engine.

run-llama/llama_index#20271.body:
### Feature Description

qdrant-client version 1.16.0 has been released, but LlamaIndex does not currently support it. After upgrading qdrant-client, code that previously worked normally in version 1.51.1 will now throw an error when initializing the local client in `async_qdrant_local.py`:

```
    async_client = AsyncQdrantClient(
                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\qdrant_client\async_qdrant_client.py", line 124, in __init__
    self._client = AsyncQdrantLocal(
                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\qdrant_client\local\async_qdrant_local.py", line 66, in __init__
    self._load()
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\qdrant_client\local\async_qdrant_local.py", line 103, in _load
    config = rest_models.CreateCollection(**config_json)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\pydantic\main.py", line 250, in __init__
    validated_self = self.__pydantic_validator__.validate_python(data, self_instance=self)
                     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for CreateCollection
init_from
  Extra inputs are not permitted [type=extra_forbidden, input_value=None, input_type=NoneType]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.12/v/extra_forbidden
```

If using the remote client, an error occurs during retrieval stating that the client does not have a `search` attribute:

```
   results = await retriever_tool.retriever.aretrieve(query_bundle)
              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\llama_index_instrumentation\dispatcher.py", line 368, in async_wrapper
    result = await func(*args, **kwargs)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\llama_index\core\base\base_retriever.py", line 241, in aretrieve
    nodes = await self._aretrieve(query_bundle=query_bundle)
            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\llama_index_instrumentation\dispatcher.py", line 368, in async_wrapper
    result = await func(*args, **kwargs)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\llama_index\core\indices\vector_store\retrievers\retriever.py", line 115, in _aretrieve
    return await self._aget_nodes_with_embeddings(
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\llama_index\core\indices\vector_store\retrievers\retriever.py", line 241, in _aget_nodes_with_embeddings
    query_result = await self._vector_store.aquery(query, **self._kwargs)
                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\LlamaIndexQwen\venv\Lib\site-packages\llama_index\vector_stores\qdrant\base.py", line 1310, in aquery
    response = await self._aclient.search(
                     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
AttributeError: 'AsyncQdrantClient' object has no attribute 'search'
```

### Reason

The main blocker is that the new `qdrant-client 1.16.0` introduced breaking changes to its API that are incompatible with the current code in LlamaIndex. Specifically:
*   **For the local client:** The internal data model for creating collections has changed, causing a Pydantic validation error when loading existing data.
*   **For the remote client:** The `AsyncQdrantClient` object no longer has a direct `.search` method, which LlamaIndex's vector store code relies on. The existing methods in LlamaIndex that call `client.search()` are now ineffective and throw an `AttributeError`.

### Value of Feature


Supporting the new version is critical for users to benefit from:
*   **New Features & Performance:** Access to the latest features, performance improvements, and bug fixes provided by Qdrant.
*   **Security:** Ensuring compatibility with the latest, most secure version of the client library.
*   **Ecosystem Cohesion:** Preventing a version lock-in and allowing the LlamaIndex ecosystem to stay current with its dependencies.
run-llama/llama_index#20271.title: [Feature Request]: Support for the new version qdrant-client 1.16.0

run-llama/llama_index#20272.body:
### Question Validation

- [x] I have searched both the documentation and discord for an answer.

### Question

Hi again,

the same problem as a few hours ago.

No matter where or what I set as values for the timeouts, still the MCP-Client which is inside the Agents of llama-index, kills a task after 30 seconds. No matter the value after 30 seconds, the client sends a DELETE to the MCP-Server, because of that the framework eventually tries again, sometimes the agent does, sometimes it doesnt. 

The problem is simply, why (in my case, does the framework kill the session, mid-run) even though I set the timeout way higher..?

I also have a basic code of what I am doing / trying, reduced to the problematic stuff, no imports etc.

Code:
```
async def create_gitlab_mcp_tools():
    """To create a MCP-Client, convert it into a ToolSpec and return it for usage in agent."""
    personal_token_gitlab = os.getenv("GITLAB_PERSONAL_TOKEN")
    mcp_client = BasicMCPClient("http://127.0.0.1:8000/mcp", headers={"Authorization":f"Bearer {personal_token_gitlab}"}, timeout=600)
    
    mcp_tool = McpToolSpec(client=mcp_client)
    return mcp_tool

async def start_workflow(model: LLM):
    mcp_tool_gitlab = await create_gitlab_mcp_tools()
    tool_list_gitlab = await mcp_tool_gitlab.to_tool_list_async()
    
    gitlab_agent = ReActAgent(
        name="GitlabAgent",
        description="Uses the GitLab tools to do several things in GitLab.",
        llm=model,
        tools=tool_list_gitlab,
        verbose=True,
        timeout=600
    )

    orchestrator_agent = ReActAgent(
        name="OrchestratorAgent",
        description="Orchestrates the user requests to the agents to do.",
        llm=model,
        can_handoff_to=["GitlabAgent"],
        verbose=True,
        timeout=600
    )

    agent_workflow = AgentWorkflow(
        agents=[gitlab_agent, orchestrator_agent],
        root_agent=orchestrator_agent.name,
        timeout=600
    )
    
    return agent_workflow


async def main():
    dotenv.load_dotenv()
    
    model = CustomOpenAiLike(
        model="gpt-oss-120b",
        is_chat_model=True,
        timeout=600,
        is_function_calling_model=True,
        max_tokens=128000,
        context_window=128000,
        api_base="https://base-url/v1",
        api_key="x",
        token_refresh_fn=get_new_token,
        system_prompt=BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT,
    )
    
    memory = ChatMemoryBuffer.from_defaults(llm=model)
    workflow = await start_workflow(model=model)
    context = Context(workflow)
    
    while(True):
        print(Fore.BLUE + "\nPlease input your query.")
        query = input(">> ")
        
        if query in ["q", "quit", "exit"]:
            exit(0)

        resp = await workflow.run(user_msg=query, ctx=context, memory=memory)
        print("Answer of the agent:\n", str(resp))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

```

Additionally:
- The prompt works fine, the model is capable of running tools correctly, I have no problems in that regards.
- The model is a GPT OSS 120B, again everything works fine on that end.
- The custom "CustomOpenAiLike" also has nothing to do with it, it's just for authentication renewal, I switched back and forth between the vanilla version.

run-llama/llama_index#20278.body:
## Bug Description

There is a path mismatch in the VertexAI Vector Search integration that causes index updates to fail. The code uploads embeddings to one GCS path but tells VertexAI to look for them at a different path.

## Affected Component

`llama_index.vector_stores.vertexaivectorsearch.utils.py` in the `batch_update_index()` function

**Package version:** `llama-index-vector-stores-vertexaivectorsearch==0.3.1`

## Root Cause

In `utils.py`, lines 197-200:

```python
# Line 197: Uploads to gs://bucket/{prefix}/documents.json
blob = staging_bucket.blob(f"{prefix}/{file_name}")
blob.upload_from_string(file_content)

# Line 200: But tells VertexAI to look at gs://bucket/index/{prefix}/
contents_delta_uri = f"gs://{staging_bucket.name}/index/{prefix}"
```

The upload path is missing the `/index/` prefix that appears in `contents_delta_uri`.

## Expected Behavior

The paths should match. Either:
1. Upload to: `gs://bucket/index/{prefix}/documents.json`
2. Or tell VertexAI to look at: `gs://bucket/{prefix}/`

## Actual Behavior

- Files are uploaded to: `gs://bucket/{uuid}/documents.json`
- VertexAI looks for files at: `gs://bucket/index/{uuid}/`
- Index update fails with: "gs://bucket/index/{uuid} is not a valid directory or the directory has empty content"

## Reproduction

```python
from llama_index.core import Document, VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
from llama_index.vector_stores.vertexaivectorsearch import VertexAIVectorStore

# Configure VertexAI Vector Store
vector_store = VertexAIVectorStore(
    project_id="my-project",
    region="us-central1",
    index_id="projects/.../indexes/123",
    endpoint_id="projects/.../indexEndpoints/456",
    gcs_bucket_name="my-bucket",
)

storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)

# Create documents
documents = [Document(text="Test document")]

# This will fail due to path mismatch
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    storage_context=storage_context,
)
```

Error:
```
ValueError: `gs://my-bucket/index/{uuid}` is not a valid directory or the directory has empty content
```

## Workaround

We've implemented a workaround that manually copies files after the failed upload:

```python
try:
    index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents, storage_context=storage_context)
except Exception as e:
    if "is not a valid directory" in str(e):
        # Extract UUID from error
        import re
        match = re.search(r"gs://[^/]+/index/([a-f0-9-]+)", str(e))
        if match:
            uuid_prefix = match.group(1)
            
            # Copy files from gs://bucket/{uuid}/ to gs://bucket/index/{uuid}/
            from google.cloud import storage
            client = storage.Client()
            bucket = client.bucket("my-bucket")
            
            for blob in bucket.list_blobs(prefix=f"{uuid_prefix}/"):
                dest_name = f"index/{blob.name}"
                bucket.copy_blob(blob, bucket, dest_name)
            
            # Retry
            index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents, storage_context=storage_context)
```

## Proposed Fix

**Option 1 (Recommended):** Fix the upload path to include `/index/`:

```python
# Line 197 should be:
blob = staging_bucket.blob(f"index/{prefix}/{file_name}")
```

**Option 2:** Fix the contents_delta_uri to remove `/index/`:

```python
# Line 200 should be:
contents_delta_uri = f"gs://{staging_bucket.name}/{prefix}"
```

I recommend **Option 1** because it's more explicit and matches the documented pattern.

## Impact

This bug affects anyone using VertexAI Vector Search with LlamaIndex. The index creation will fail unless users:
1. Manually copy files to the correct location
2. Configure their VertexAI index to point to `gs://bucket/` instead of `gs://bucket/index/`

## Environment

- `llama-index-core`: 0.14.7
- `llama-index-vector-stores-vertexaivectorsearch`: 0.3.1
- Python: 3.11
- GCP VertexAI Matching Engine

## Additional Context

This issue is particularly confusing because:
1. The error message doesn't clearly indicate a path mismatch
2. Users must configure their VertexAI index to point to `gs://bucket/index/` to work around this
3. The workaround (copying files) adds complexity and potential race conditions
run-llama/llama_index#20278.title: [Bug] VertexAI Vector Search: GCS path mismatch between upload and index update

### Bug Description

The structured prediction with Gemini models over the `llama-index-llms-google-genai` integration only works for `gemini-2.5-pro`, but not for the following models:
- `gemini-2.5-flash`
- `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`
- `gemini-2.0-flash`
- `gemini-2.0-flash-lite`

It raises the error:
> ValueError: Response is not a BaseModel

I can see that the model generated valid data in `response.text`, but the `response.parsed` field is `None`.

This is handled in https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/6bf76bf1ca5c70e479a3adb3327cf896cbcd869f/llama-index-integrations/llms/llama-index-llms-google-genai/llama_index/llms/google_genai/base.py#L619-L622

### Version

llama-index-core==0.14.8, llama-index-llms-google-genai==0.7.3, google-genai==1.52.0

```python

from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI

from pydantic import BaseModel, UUID4

run-llama/llama_index#20324.body:
### Bug Description

Using a generic `BaseModel` with `as_structured_llm` causes a 400 BadRequest from OpenAI.
This is probably due to the generated JSON schema name.




### Version

0.14.8

### Steps to Reproduce

The following code works correctly with a non-generic model (`DataModel`) but fails when using the generic version (`GenericDataModel[int]`):

```python
from typing import Sequence
from llama_index.core.base.llms.types import ChatMessage
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel

class DataModel(BaseModel):
    answer: int

class GenericDataModel[T](BaseModel):
    answer: T

def structured_output_existential_chat(datamodel):
    llm = OpenAI(model="gpt-4o")
    sllm = llm.as_structured_llm(output_cls=datamodel)

    messages: Sequence[ChatMessage] = [ChatMessage(content="What is the meaning of life?")]
    response = sllm.chat(messages)
    print(response)

structured_output_existential_chat(DataModel)

print(GenericDataModel[int].model_json_schema())
structured_output_existential_chat(GenericDataModel[int])
```

**Observed behavior**

```
assistant: {"answer":42}

{'properties': {'answer': {'title': 'Answer', 'type': 'integer'}}, 'required': ['answer'], 'title': 'GenericDataModel[int]', 'type': 'object'}
```

Then:

```

### Bug Description

## Description

In commit 71e1f9e (file: 
`llama-index-integrations/vector_stores/llama-index-vector-stores-faiss/llama_index/vector_stores/faiss/map_store.py`), the code calls:

```python
self._faiss_index.add_with_ids(text_embedding_np, self._faiss_index.ntotal)


self._faiss_index.ntotal is an int. According to faiss IndexIDMap.add_with_ids API, the second argument should be a 1-D numpy array of int64 ids (shape (n,), dtype np.int64) matching the number of vectors. Passing a plain int may raise an error or produce undefined behavior.




### Version

0.14.6

### Steps to Reproduce

reader = SimpleDirectoryReader([filepath])
document = reader.load_data[0]
splitter= SentenceSplitter()
nodes = splitter.get_nodes_from_documents([document])
d = 128
index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(d, 32)
id_map_index = faiss.IndexIDMap2(index)
vector_store = FaissMapVectorStore(id_map_index )
storage_context - StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)
index = VectorStoreIndex(nodes=nodes, storage_context=storage_context )

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell
file: .../faiss/class_wrappers.py line 260
assert ids.shape ==(n, ) not same nb of vectors as ids
AttributeError: int object has no attribute shape
```

run-llama/llama_index#20376.body:
### Feature Description

In order to break the [P1 Issue](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/issues/15667) of supporting multimodal ChatEngines/QueryEngines, this issue address the preliminary work of supporting ChatMessage inputs to a `ChatPromptHelper` class. 

### Reason

- Preliminary work for a P1 issue
- Helps other users who have requested similar functionality
- Supports our current needs as well

### Value of Feature

Closes the gap between the libraries support for multimodal retrieval (currently text and images) and mutlimodal input to LLMs (which currently supports a broad range of content types) by supporting multimodal chat prompts in classes which are auxiliary to Synthesis and NodePostprocessing, which are core components of ChatEngine/QueryEngine classes.

run-llama/llama_index#20461.body:
### Bug Description

When calling `mean_agg` with an empty list of embeddings, the function returns a float `nan` (produced by NumPy) instead of an empty list or raising a clear error. 

This violates the function's type hint `-> Embedding` (which is an alias for `List[float]`). Since the return type changes from a `list` to a `float`, any downstream logic that expects a list (e.g., calling `len()`, iterating, or indexing) will raise a `TypeError`, leading to hard-to-debug crashes.


### Version

llama-index-core==0.14.12

### Steps to Reproduce
```python
from llama_index.core.base.embeddings.base import mean_agg

embeddings = []
result = mean_agg(embeddings)

print("Checking result for empty input:", result) # nan
print("Type of result:", type(result)) # <class 'float'>
```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20461.title: [Bug]: `mean_agg` returns `nan` (float) instead of a list when input is an empty list

run-llama/llama_index#20471.body:
### Feature Description

GitHubRepoReader currently reads the entire repository for a given branch. While it supports excluding files or file extensions, it does not allow selectively fetching specific files based on file paths, filenames, or commit SHAs.

This Enhancement proposes:

Adding support to fetch only specific files (by path, name, or SHA) instead of scanning the whole repository.

Introducing an optional mechanism (e.g., a docstore or cache check) to determine whether a file has already been read before fetching it again.

This would reduce unnecessary reads, avoid redundant processing, and significantly improve performance for large repositories or incremental updates.

### Reason

_No response_

### Value of Feature

This enhancement enables more fine-grained and efficient integration with GitHubRepoReader.
By avoiding full repository scans and redundant file reads, it improves performance, reduces API usage, and makes the reader far more suitable for real-world workflows like incremental indexing, selective updates, and large mono-repos.
run-llama/llama_index#20471.title: Enhancement of GitHubRepoReader

run-llama/llama_index#20488.body:
### Bug Description

Since https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/pull/19444, the default nltk_data path was changed for security reasons.
```
- self._nltk_data_dir = os.environ.get(
-     "NLTK_DATA",
-     os.path.join(
-         os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)),
-         "_static/nltk_cache",
-     ),
- )
+ if "NLTK_DATA" in os.environ:
+     path = Path(os.environ["NLTK_DATA"])
+ else:
+     path = Path(platformdirs.user_cache_dir("llama_index"))
+
+ self._nltk_data_dir = str(path / "_static/nltk_cache")
```
However, this change causes nltk resources to be re-downloaded in Docker or other sandboxed / ephemeral environments.

In intranet or restricted-network environments, this download attempt can block the program for 15 minutes or longer, effectively making the application unusable.

Previously, the packaged _static/nltk_cache under llama_index/core/_static could be reused directly, but with the new path logic, this cache is no longer picked up automatically.

**Impact**

1. Docker, CI, and other sandboxed environments re-trigger nltk downloads
2. Programs may hang for a long time in intranet / offline environments
3. Users are forced to manually configure NLTK_DATA, which is non-obvious

**Expected Behavior**

If nltk data already exists in llama_index/core/_static/nltk_cache, it should be reused without triggering a download, especially when no explicit NLTK_DATA is set.

**Suggested Solution**

At minimum, consider one of the following approaches:

0. **Add a timeout to the nltk download / availability check, so the program does not block indefinitely in offline or intranet environments**
1. Copy or initialize the packaged llama_index/core/_static/nltk_cache into the new user cache directory on first run
2. Add a fallback that checks the packaged _static cache before attempting a download
3. Provide a clearer default behavior for offline / sandboxed environments
4. This would preserve the security improvements while avoiding unexpected blocking behavior.

### Version

v0.12.52.post1

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Build an intranet or restricted-network environment (e.g. Docker) with llama-index and llama-index-core
2. Start a Python session and run:
3. 
```
from llama_index.core.utils import globals_helper
globals_helper.wait_for_nltk_check()
```
    - from llama_index.core.utils import globals_helper
    - globals_helper.wait_for_nltk_check()
3. Observe that the process blocks while attempting to re-download nltk data due to the new cache path

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20488.title: [Bug]: nltk-data is re-downloaded after path change, causing long blocking in sandboxed environments

run-llama/llama_index#20498.body:
## Feature Request

Add a LlamaIndex tool for MCP Discovery API, enabling agents to dynamically discover MCP servers.

## Use Case

LlamaIndex agents could discover tools on-the-fly:

```python
from llama_index.tools import MCPDiscoveryTool

discovery_tool = MCPDiscoveryTool(
    api_url="https://mcp-discovery-production.up.railway.app"
)

# Agent discovers the best tool for the task
agent = ReActAgent.from_tools([discovery_tool, ...])
response = agent.chat("Help me set up a database for my project")
# Agent discovers and recommends Supabase/PostgreSQL MCP servers
```

## What MCP Discovery provides

- Semantic search across 24+ MCP servers
- Performance metrics (latency, uptime)
- Natural language queries
- Free tier (100 queries/month)

## Links

- GitHub: https://github.com/yksanjo/mcp-discovery
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/mcp-discovery-api
- Live API: https://mcp-discovery-production.up.railway.app

Happy to contribute a PR!
run-llama/llama_index#20498.title: [Feature Request] Add MCP Discovery tool for dynamic server discovery

run-llama/llama_index#20506.body:
### Bug Description

When using Anthropic llm, in `stream_chat` and `astream_chat`, the information regarding input tokens is lost in the final response.

I can already propose a fix, and I am open to do a PR.
Inside the `gen` method of `astream_chat` (and the same logic applies to `gen` method of `stream_chat`):
```python
# llama_index/llms/anthropic/base.py
...
# Track usage metadata and stop_reason from RawMessage events
usage_metadata: Dict[str, Any] = {}
input_tokens: Optional[int] = None
stop_reason: Optional[str] = None
for r in response:
...
  elif isinstance(r, RawMessageStartEvent):
    # Capture initial usage metadata from message_start
    if hasattr(r.message, "usage") and r.message.usage:
        # Save input tokens for later
        input_tokens = r.message.usage.input_tokens
        usage_metadata = {
            "input_tokens": r.message.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": r.message.usage.output_tokens,
        }
  elif isinstance(r, RawMessageDeltaEvent):
    # Update usage metadata and capture stop_reason from message_delta
    if hasattr(r, "usage") and r.usage:
        # Modify r.usage.input_tokens if None with saved input tokens value
        r.usage.input_tokens = r.usage.input_tokens or input_tokens
        usage_metadata = {
            "input_tokens": r.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": r.usage.output_tokens,
        }

```

### Version

0.14.2

### Steps to Reproduce

run-llama/llama_index#20530.body:
### Bug Description

### Summary
When using `llama-index-llms-dashscope` with ReAct/tool workflows, the adapter function that converts `ChatMessage` objects to DashScope request payloads always includes a `tool_calls` field for **every** `assistant` message. If the message has no tool calls, it sends `tool_calls: []`.

Some Qwen/DashScope backends reject `tool_calls` when it is present but empty, which can cause the request to fail (or return an empty/invalid response). In LlamaIndex agent workflows this may surface as `ValueError: Got empty message` after a tool is invoked.

### Environment
- OS: macOS (also likely affects other OSes)
- Python: 3.12
- `llama-index`: 0.14.13
- `llama-index-llms-dashscope`: 0.5.1
- LLM: DashScope / Qwen (`model`: from `QIANWEN_MODEL` env var)

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Configure LlamaIndex to use DashScope as the LLM.
2. Create any tool (e.g., `QueryEngineTool`) and use `ReActAgent` (or any tool-using agent/workflow).
3. Ask a question that triggers a tool call.
4. After tool execution, the agent attempts to call the LLM again and fails because the DashScope request contains `tool_calls: []` on assistant messages that are not tool calls.

### Expected Behavior
For assistant messages that do **not** contain tool calls, the DashScope request payload should **not include** the `tool_calls` field at all.

### Actual Behavior
The DashScope integration always includes:

```json
{
  "role": "assistant",
  "content": "...",
  "tool_calls": []
}
```
### Root cause
file:llama_index/llms/dashscope/utils.py
bug function:
```
def chat_message_to_dashscope_messages(
    chat_messages: Sequence[ChatMessage],
) -> List[Dict]:
    messages = []
    for msg in chat_messages:
        additional_kwargs = msg.additional_kwargs
        if msg.role == "assistant":
            messages.append(
                {
                    "role": msg.role.value,
                    "content": msg.content,
                    "tool_calls": additional_kwargs.get("tool_calls", []),
                }
            )
        elif msg.role == "tool":
            messages.append(
                {
                    "role": msg.role.value,
                    "content": msg.content,
                    "tool_call_id": additional_kwargs.get("tool_call_id", ""),
                    "name": additional_kwargs.get("name", ""),
                }
            )
        else:
            messages.append({"role": msg.role.value, "content": msg.content})
    return messages
```
Proposed Fix
Only include tool_calls when it exists and is non-empty:
```
def chat_message_to_dashscope_messages(
    chat_messages: Sequence[ChatMessage],
) -> List[Dict]:
    messages = []
    for msg in chat_messages:
        additional_kwargs = msg.additional_kwargs
        if msg.role == "assistant":
            m = {"role": msg.role.value, "content": msg.content}
            tool_calls = additional_kwargs.get("tool_calls")
            if tool_calls:  
                m["tool_calls"] = tool_calls
            messages.append(m)
        elif msg.role == "tool":
            messages.append(
                {
                    "role": msg.role.value,
                    "content": msg.content,
                    "tool_call_id": additional_kwargs.get("tool_call_id", ""),
                    "name": additional_kwargs.get("name", ""),
                }
            )
        else:
            messages.append({"role": msg.role.value, "content": msg.content})
    return messages
```



### Version

0.5.1

### Steps to Reproduce

    os.environ["SSL_CERT_FILE"] = certifi.where()
    from pinecone import Pinecone
    from llama_index.core.workflow import Context

run-llama/llama_index#20533.body:
### Bug Description

Just like Anthropic class has an option to define a base_url, the Cohere llm class is missing this. When initializing the Cohere llm, we should be able to pass a custom base_url like an endpoint obtained from an llm provider which is different from the default base_url.

This is the code which needs update:

```
class Cohere(FunctionCallingLLM):
    """
    Cohere LLM.

    Examples:
        `pip install llama-index-llms-cohere`

        ```python
        from llama_index.llms.cohere import Cohere

        llm = Cohere(model="command", api_key=api_key)
        resp = llm.complete("Paul Graham is ")
        print(resp)
        ```

    """

    model: str = Field(description="The cohere model to use.")
    temperature: Optional[float] = Field(
        description="The temperature to use for sampling.", default=None
    )
    max_retries: int = Field(
        default=10, description="The maximum number of API retries."
    )
    additional_kwargs: Dict[str, Any] = Field(
        default_factory=dict, description="Additional kwargs for the Cohere API."
    )
    max_tokens: int = Field(description="The maximum number of tokens to generate.")

    _client: Any = PrivateAttr()
    _aclient: Any = PrivateAttr()

    def __init__(
        self,
        model: str = "command-r",
        temperature: Optional[float] = None,
        max_tokens: Optional[int] = 8192,
        timeout: Optional[float] = None,
        max_retries: int = 10,
        api_key: Optional[str] = None,
        additional_kwargs: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None,
        callback_manager: Optional[CallbackManager] = None,
        system_prompt: Optional[str] = None,
        messages_to_prompt: Optional[Callable[[Sequence[ChatMessage]], str]] = None,
        completion_to_prompt: Optional[Callable[[str], str]] = None,
        pydantic_program_mode: PydanticProgramMode = PydanticProgramMode.DEFAULT,
        output_parser: Optional[BaseOutputParser] = None,
    ) -> None:
        additional_kwargs = additional_kwargs or {}
        callback_manager = callback_manager or CallbackManager([])

        super().__init__(
            temperature=temperature,
            additional_kwargs=additional_kwargs,
            timeout=timeout,
            max_retries=max_retries,
            model=model,
            callback_manager=callback_manager,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            system_prompt=system_prompt,
            messages_to_prompt=messages_to_prompt,
            completion_to_prompt=completion_to_prompt,
            pydantic_program_mode=pydantic_program_mode,
            output_parser=output_parser,
        )
        self._client = cohere.Client(api_key, client_name="llama_index")
        self._aclient = cohere.AsyncClient(api_key, client_name="llama_index")
```

### Version

0.14.8

        "base_url": baseUrl
    }
    return Cohere(**config)

run-llama/llama_index#20558.body:
### Bug Description

It is not possible to initialize an on-prem Nvidia Reranker that requires an API key.

Library: llama-index-postprocessor-nvidia-rerank==0.5.1

This is due to the line `165`, which only ever sets `auth_required` to `True` for hosted Nvidia models:
https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/eda20206a2750d76cd37428d5df9570a6cc5f301/llama-index-integrations/postprocessor/llama-index-postprocessor-nvidia-rerank/llama_index/postprocessor/nvidia_rerank/base.py#L163-L172

Instead of checking for `self._api_key`, the code uses `self._is_hosted` as the way to determine if authorization is required or not. This results in the auth header never being set.
https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/eda20206a2750d76cd37428d5df9570a6cc5f301/llama-index-integrations/postprocessor/llama-index-postprocessor-nvidia-rerank/llama_index/postprocessor/nvidia_rerank/base.py#L152-L161

This could likely be solved with the following code: 
```
_headers = self._get_headers(auth_required=bool(self._api_key or self._is_hosted))
```


### Version

llama-index==0.14.13 | llama-index-postprocessor-nvidia-rerank==0.5.1

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Initialize an Nvidia Reranker that requires an API key/token using the following params: model, api_key, and base_url.

Actual result: The API key isn't passed into the HTTP header, which causes an exception being thrown when looping through the available models.

Expected result: The API key should be passed into the headers.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```

run-llama/llama_index#20575.body:
### Bug Description

Hi,

I saw https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/issues/19924 and https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/issues/19288 but these didnt went anywhere.

I have the same problem and try to deliver informations which could give you enough to eventually solve it.

**What model / technical configuration:**
- Kimi-K2 (locally hosted) as well as GPT OSS 120B (both fail from time to time).
- MCP-Tools and Servers (only one exposed at a time for testing, few tools so not much context usage)
- Local MCP-Server so no connection problems at all

**What I know:**
This happens quite randomly, sometimes when I dont enter anything and then e.g. five minutes later I enter something and it is down or sometimes in the middle, no reason nothing.

**What did I do:**
User Input was "try 20", before the input was 7, 14 and other numbers and the whole thing worked fine (MCP-Tool execution with feedback to framework/agent to talk about received informations). After inputting "try 20" the thing simply crashed.



### Version

llama-cloud==0.1.35 llama-cloud-services==0.6.54 llama-index==0.14.12 llama-index-cli==0.5.3 llama-index-core==0.14.12 llama-index-embeddings-openai==0.5.1 llama-index-indices-managed-llama-cloud==0.9.4 llama-index-instrumentation==0.4.2 llama-index-llms-openai==0.6.12 llama-index-llms-openai-like==0.5.3 llama-index-readers-file==0.5.6 llama-index-readers-llama-parse==0.5.1 llama-index-tools-mcp==0.4.5 llama-index-workflows==2.11.1 llama-parse==0.6.54

You have have a continous conversation back and forth, at some point the model will (as it seems like), give back a empty message and this crashes the framework directly. I dont have any other clue.

run-llama/llama_index#20583.body:
### Feature Description

The Wolfram|Alpha API provides many different URL parameters, one of which includes `format` which has an option for `plaintext` : https://products.wolframalpha.com/api/documentation?scrollTo=parameter-reference. However, the `wolfram_alpha` agent hard-codes the parameters:

https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/05c2b089dcce1d55169bda2dffd2bce4f7e2af96/llama-index-integrations/tools/llama-index-tools-wolfram-alpha/llama_index/tools/wolfram_alpha/base.py

I can work around this by subclassing or monkeypatching, but I'd prefer not to. Ideally, I'd like to see this accept kwargs and build the URL programmatically.

### Reason

N/A

### Value of Feature

Flexibility to provide any Wolfram|Alpha API argument present in their official documentation, rather than LlamaIndex's opinionated API call. https://products.wolframalpha.com/api/documentation?scrollTo=parameter-reference
run-llama/llama_index#20583.title: [Feature Request]: Customize URL parameters for the `wolfram_alpha` tool

run-llama/llama_index#20584.body:
### Feature Description

Add Moss as a Tool to enable low-latency, local-first semantic search within agents and chains.

Moss is a real-time semantic search engine optimized for conversational and multimodal AI. It enables agents, voice assistants, and chatbots to retrieve, reason, and respond in sub-10ms by colocating the vector search index with the model in the browser, on-device, or on the server. It supports hybrid keyword + semantic retrieval and comes with JS/Python SDKs.

This feature would expose Moss as a Tool that agents can call (e.g., moss_search("query")) to:

- retrieve top-k results using Moss’s low-latency semantic engine,
- return rich text snippets or document metadata for downstream reasoning,
- optionally blend keyword and vector search with parameter control.

Available at https://usemoss.dev/



### Reason

Agents often rely on external vector databases for retrieval (e.g. FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate), but these typically introduce:

High latency (100–1000ms per query),
Network overhead (API calls, remote hosts),
Limited edge compatibility (can’t run in-browser or offline).
Moss solves this by providing an embedded semantic search runtime with:



### Value of Feature

- <10ms retrieval (in-browser, mobile, or local server),
- lightweight integration (tiny SDKs + no infra),
- hybrid scoring (semantic + keyword),
run-llama/llama_index#20584.title: [Feature Request]: Addition of Moss for realtime Conversational AI

run-llama/llama_index#20585.body:
### Bug Description

I was trying to use the OpenAI-Like client to integrate a vision model hosted at an OVH Public Cloud AI Endpoint and the model constantly returned with 422 as the provided message including my image was not processable.

After some investigation I figured out that the OVH inference API does not accept the "detail" field inside an image block which is in line with the OpenAI documentation here: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/images-vision#specify-image-input-detail-level . Patching the creation of the content block in llamaindex fixed my issue but I would like to ask the llamaindex project if removing your default of setting this value to "auto" can be removed from the official code base to be in line with the OpenAI documentation too. On a first glance it looks like a quick win and allows users to use OVH hosted vision models too.

The relevant code in llamaindex is inside to_openai_message_dict of the openai utils.py (https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/main/llama-index-integrations/llms/llama-index-llms-openai/llama_index/llms/openai/utils.py) around lines 555ff.

The fix is to not set "detail" at all when block.detail is None.

### Version

main branch

### Steps to Reproduce

- Host Mistral-Small-3.2-24B-Instruct-2506 in OVH Public Cloud (https://www.ovhcloud.com/en/public-cloud/ai-endpoints/catalog/mistral-small-3-2-24b-instruct-2506/)
- Create an OpenAILike llm client
- Send a message including a text block and an image block

Observed: http response 422
Expected: Valid response from the model

To understand the model behavior, use this for testing:

### Not working request (llamaindex openai utils request):

        ]

}

print(f"Status: {resp.status_code}")
print(f"Body: {resp.text}")
```

#### Response

Status: 422
Body: Failed to deserialize the JSON body into the target type: messages[0]: data did not match any variant of untagged enum ChatMessageContent at line 1 column 267
```

### Bug Description

Looking at the implementation of `llm_retry_decorator` in
`llama_index.llms.google_genai`, it appears that async methods are not handled
correctly.

Specifically, the decorator always returns a synchronous wrapper and does not
check whether the wrapped function is a coroutine.

```python
def llm_retry_decorator(f: Callable[..., Any]) -> Callable[..., Any]:
    @functools.wraps(f)
    def wrapper(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any:
        max_retries = getattr(self, "max_retries", 0)
        if max_retries <= 0:
            return f(self, *args, **kwargs)

        retry = create_retry_decorator(
            max_retries=max_retries,
            random_exponential=True,
            stop_after_delay_seconds=60,
            min_seconds=1,
            max_seconds=20,
        )
        return retry(f)(self, *args, **kwargs)

    return wrapper


### Version

llama-index-llms-google-genai = 0.8.4

### Steps to Reproduce

``` python
import asyncio
from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI
```

Check if the decorated method is async
``` python
print(asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(GoogleGenAI._achat))
```
The  _achat method is an async function but iscoroutinefunction() returns False because the decorator wraps it with a sync wrapper.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#20587.title: [Bug]: llm_retry_decorator do not supports async methods

run-llama/llama_index#20597.body:
### Bug Description

# [Bug]: TEI Rerank postprocessor incorrectly assigns scores to nodes

## Bug Description

The `TextEmbeddingInference` reranker in `llama-index-postprocessor-tei-rerank` incorrectly assigns rerank scores to nodes. The current implementation assumes that the TEI `/rerank` API returns scores in the same order as the input documents, but according to the [HuggingFace TEI OpenAPI specification](https://huggingface.github.io/text-embeddings-inference/openapi.json), the API returns results **sorted by score in descending order**, with an `index` field indicating the original position of each document.

## Version

- `llama-index-postprocessor-tei-rerank`: 0.3.0
- `llama-index-core`: 0.12.36

## Steps to Reproduce

```python
from llama_index.core.schema import NodeWithScore, TextNode
from llama_index.postprocessor.tei_rerank import TextEmbeddingInference as TEIR

# Test data
TEST_QUERY = "What is machine learning?"
TEST_DOCUMENTS = [
    "Machine learning is a branch of AI that enables computers to learn from data.",  # index=0, highly relevant
    "The weather is nice today, perfect for going out.",  # index=1, irrelevant
    "Deep learning is a subset of machine learning using neural networks.",  # index=2, relevant
    "Python is a popular programming language.",  # index=3, somewhat relevant
    "Supervised learning requires labeled data to train models.",  # index=4, relevant
]

# Create TEIR reranker
teir = TEIR(
    base_url="http://your-tei-server:8080",
    top_n=5,
)

# Build nodes
nodes = [
    NodeWithScore(node=TextNode(text=doc), score=0.0)
    for doc in TEST_DOCUMENTS
]

# Execute reranking
reranked_nodes = teir.postprocess_nodes(nodes, query_str=TEST_QUERY)

# Print results
for i, node in enumerate(reranked_nodes):
    print(f"[{i+1}] score={node.score:.6f}, text={node.node.get_content()[:50]}...")
```

## Expected Behavior

The irrelevant document "The weather is nice today..." (index=1) should receive the **lowest** score (close to 0).

Expected output order (by relevance):
1. Machine learning is a branch of AI... (highest score ~0.999)
2. Deep learning is a subset of machine learning... (score ~0.158)
3. Supervised learning requires labeled data... (score ~0.0065)
4. Python is a popular programming language... (score ~0.003)
5. The weather is nice today... (lowest score ~0.00001)

## Actual Behavior

The scores are **incorrectly assigned** to wrong nodes. For example, "The weather is nice today..." receives a high score of ~0.158, while "Deep learning..." receives a much lower score.

Actual output:
```
[1] index=0, score=0.998910, text=Machine learning is a branch of AI...
[2] index=1, score=0.158304, text=The weather is nice today...  <-- WRONG! Should be lowest
[3] index=2, score=0.006539, text=Deep learning is a subset of machine learning...
[4] index=3, score=0.003016, text=Python is a popular programming language...
[5] index=4, score=0.000016, text=Supervised learning requires labeled data...
```

## Root Cause Analysis

The TEI `/rerank` API returns results in this format (sorted by score descending):

```json
[
  {"index": 0, "score": 0.99890983},
  {"index": 2, "score": 0.15830442},
  {"index": 4, "score": 0.0065388246},
  {"index": 3, "score": 0.0030160663},
  {"index": 1, "score": 0.000016187581}
]
```

The `index` field indicates which original document each score belongs to.

However, the current code at `base.py` line 147:

```python
for node, score in zip(nodes, scores):
    ...
    node.score = float(score["score"])
```

This incorrectly assumes `scores` is in the same order as `nodes`, using `zip` to pair them directly. It ignores the `index` field entirely.

## Suggested Fix

Use the `index` field from the API response to correctly assign scores:

```python
for score_item in scores:
    original_index = score_item["index"]
    if self.keep_retrieval_score:
        nodes[original_index].node.metadata["retrieval_score"] = nodes[original_index].score
    nodes[original_index].score = float(score_item["score"])
```

## Additional Context

- TEI API Documentation: https://huggingface.github.io/text-embeddings-inference/
- The `Rank` schema in OpenAPI spec clearly defines: `index` (integer) - the original position, `score` (float) - the relevance score


### Version

0.12.36

### Steps to Reproduce

for score_item in scores:
    original_index = score_item["index"]
    if self.keep_retrieval_score:
        nodes[original_index].node.metadata["retrieval_score"] = nodes[original_index].score
    nodes[original_index].score = float(score_item["score"])

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
